skill.name: security-scanning-security-dependencies
skill.description: Audit project dependencies for vulnerabilities, license risk, SBOM needs, and remediation plans across npm, Python, Go, and Rust.
skill.summary: Find vulnerable dependencies and plan safer remediation across common package ecosystems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review found no malicious intent or prompt injection. The confirmed risk is operational: the playbook includes examples that execute package scanners, install tools, and run dependency update commands. Several critical static hits, including Windows SAM and command-substitution alerts, are false positives from benign code examples.

medium_findings.title: External Scanner Execution Requires User Approval
medium_findings.description: The playbook shows subprocess calls to npm audit, safety, govulncheck, and cargo audit. The commands use static argument arrays, so command injection is not evident, but they execute local tooling against the workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct subprocess calls are present in executable examples. The arguments are fixed lists, which lowers injection risk but still confirms external command execution.

medium_findings.title: Automated Remediation Can Modify Dependency State
medium_findings.description: The remediation examples include npm update, pip-audit --fix, go get -u, go mod tidy, cargo update, and an auto-fix usage example. These are legitimate security workflows, but they can change lockfiles, dependency versions, and build behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands are explicit dependency update or fix commands. The skill also warns against auto-fix without approval, so this is a managed operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Tool Installation And Registry Access Are Expected
medium_findings.description: The playbook includes commands to install scanners and references vulnerability reporting formats. These steps commonly contact package registries or remote sources, so users should approve network access and pin trusted tools where possible.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The installation commands strongly imply network activity, and the SARIF schema URL is a benign hardcoded URL. No data exfiltration endpoint was found.

low_findings.title: Critical Sensitive File Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Windows SAM findings point to shell variable and case-statement text, not credential database access. No evidence of reading Windows SAM files or credential stores was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain ECOSYSTEM handling and case labels. They do not reference SAM paths, registry hives, or credential extraction behavior.

low_findings.title: Template And System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Syntax
low_findings.description: The template-injection alert is GitHub Actions matrix syntax, and the system reconnaissance alert is a grep count in a CI threshold check. No prompt injection or hostile reconnaissance instruction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a CI workflow example. The flagged syntax is normal YAML and shell used to count vulnerability severities.

low_findings.title: SKILL.md Command Alerts Are Resource References
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md findings point to inline code formatting around the implementation playbook path. They do not execute commands or ask the model to bypass security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are documentation references to a local resource file. No shell invocation or hidden instruction is present on those lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-Based Security Scanner Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook demonstrates subprocess.run for package security scanners. This pattern is acceptable for a scanning skill only with user approval, clear working directory scope, and timeouts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess pattern is explicit and repeated. The examples use static commands and timeouts, reducing but not removing operational risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based Dependency Update Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The bash example can update npm, Python, and Go dependencies and run tests. It should be treated as a change-making script rather than a read-only scanner.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell script contains direct package manager update commands. Its scope is transparent and domain-appropriate, but it can materially alter a project.

content.value_statement: Dependency vulnerabilities and license issues are hard to prioritize across mixed technology stacks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through scanner selection, SBOM planning, risk ranking, and remediation steps.
actual_capabilities: Identify npm, Python, Go, and Rust dependency manifests for vulnerability review.
Recommend scanner commands such as npm audit, pip-audit, govulncheck, and cargo audit.
Explain vulnerability severity, fixed versions, and remediation priority.
Outline SBOM generation using CycloneDX style reporting concepts.
Provide CI workflow patterns for scheduled dependency checks.
Prepare human-readable dependency risk reports and remediation plans.
limitations: It depends on external scanners and their vulnerability databases.
It does not prove runtime exploitability of a vulnerable package.
Auto-fix and upgrade actions require explicit approval and testing.
Results can vary by lockfile quality, package manager, and private registry access.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Dependency Audit
use_cases.description: Review lockfiles before a release and prioritize critical or high vulnerability fixes.

use_cases.title: CI Security Gate Planning
use_cases.description: Design scheduled dependency scans and failure thresholds for a build pipeline.

use_cases.title: SBOM And Compliance Preparation
use_cases.description: Plan dependency inventory output and remediation notes for audit evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Find Dependency Manifests
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this repository for dependency manifests and lockfiles. List each ecosystem found and recommend the safest scanner for each one.

prompt_templates.title: Review Scanner Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these dependency scanner results. Group findings by severity, fixed version availability, and release risk.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Remediation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a remediation plan for these vulnerable dependencies. Separate urgent fixes, routine updates, test requirements, and rollback steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design CI Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI dependency scanning policy for npm, Python, Go, and Rust. Include schedules, thresholds, artifacts, and approval rules.

output_examples.input: Scan a Node.js service before release.
output: Detected npm lockfile and recommended npm audit for initial review.
Prioritized critical and high vulnerabilities before minor update work.
Suggested running tests after any dependency changes.

output_examples.input: Prepare an SBOM and vulnerability summary for compliance.
output: Summarized vulnerability counts by severity and affected package.
Listed remediation evidence needed for audit review.

best_practices: Run scans from a clean working tree and keep generated artifacts separate from source changes.
Get approval before running fix or update commands that modify dependencies.
Validate every dependency change with tests, review, and a rollback plan.
anti_patterns: Do not run auto-fix commands on production branches without review.
Do not treat scanner severity as the only remediation priority.
Do not ignore lockfiles, private registries, or transitive dependency context.

faq.question: Does this skill run scanners automatically?
faq.answer: It provides scanner guidance and examples. Command execution should require explicit user approval.

faq.question: Which ecosystems are covered?
faq.answer: The playbook covers npm, Python, Go, and Rust dependency scanning patterns.

faq.question: Can it generate an SBOM?
faq.answer: It can guide SBOM planning and reporting. Actual SBOM generation depends on installed tools and project data.

faq.question: Is it safe for automated remediation?
faq.answer: Use caution. Auto-fix commands can change versions and lockfiles, so they need approval and tests.

faq.question: Does it verify exploitability?
faq.answer: No. It prioritizes known dependency vulnerabilities, but runtime exploitability needs separate analysis.

faq.question: Can it be used in Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
